articles.title: Ciclo económico y precio de la vivienda en España
articles.author: Fedea – Fundación de Estudios de Economía Aplicada
articles.summary:
En esta edición del Observatorio del Ciclo se aborda el estudio de los determinantes del ciclo económico en España, con la información completa hasta el último trimestre de 2025. Aparte de las secciones habituales, ponemos el foco en [...]                            
Delivered by PolitePaul service
articles.reason: Análisis ciclo económico y vivienda España

articles.title: Many Victorian cities grew by tenfold in a century
articles.author: Samuel Hughes
articles.summary: Victorian cities grew quickly and became bigger, better, and more connected with wide streets and public transport. Their growth was supported by a unique system where governments allowed profitable building and funded infrastructure through fees. This mix of planning and regulation helped create valuable neighborhoods and a large supply of housing.
articles.reason: Crecimiento urbano victoriano como modelo YIMBY

articles.title: El porcentaje de 'millennials' con casa en propiedad a los 39 años es el mismo que el de los 'X' cuando tenían 25
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La generación de las dos crisis se ve ahora golpeada por la crisis de la vivienda. Su riqueza en términos reales es menos de la mitad de la que tenían sus padres a la misma edad

articles.title: El Banco de España revela que solo un grupo de edad tiene más ingresos ahora que hace 22 años
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La riqueza mediana de los hogares de más de 75 años era un 52% superior a la de los jóvenes en el año 2002. La brecha se ha disparado hasta ser ahora un 834% superior

articles.title: Vuelve la España de las casas pequeñas: las promotoras sacrifican metros para ajustar en precio
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: In Spain, salaries are too low to afford current housing prices, so builders are making smaller homes to fit family budgets. The average size of new apartments is shrinking, especially in expensive areas like Madrid, reflecting a growing gap between wages and housing costs. There are not enough new homes being built to meet demand, which keeps prices high and worsens the housing crisis.
articles.reason: Adaptación de mercado a restricciones artificiales

articles.title: EU barriers to scaling up: The case of fragmented product markets
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: EU firms face higher trade barriers within Europe than US firms do across states. These barriers make it harder for EU companies to sell products across borders and reduce productivity. Lowering these barriers could boost EU productivity by nearly 6%.

articles.title: The Smart Second Mover, Part II
articles.author: Luis Garicano
articles.summary: Europe should focus on quickly adopting AI by creating joint ventures between foreign AI firms and European partners, supported by public procurement and a digital tax incentive. This approach, inspired by China’s auto joint ventures and Ukraine’s drone industry, aims to build local AI skills and avoid dependency on foreign companies. Open standards, decentralized buying decisions, and real market competition are key to making this work.
articles.reason: Estrategia europea para adopción rápida IA

articles.title: Too fast to adjust: Adoption speed and the permanent cost of AI transitions
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Rapid AI adoption can overwhelm worker retraining systems, causing permanent job losses and lower labor participation. When firms reorganize too quickly around AI, many displaced workers give up and leave the workforce permanently. To avoid lasting social harm, AI adoption speed must be matched with enough retraining capacity and support.
articles.reason: Costes permanentes de transición tecnológica acelerada

articles.title: The EU's path to service growth and clean tech
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: The EU can grow by improving its services sector and investing in clean and digital technologies. Stronger integration of the Single Market will boost competition and innovation. Strategic funding and policies will help the EU lead in clean tech and reduce reliance on fossil fuels.
articles.reason: Crecimiento por integración de mercado único

articles.title: Democratic economic policy in the age of AI
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: AI is changing the economy fast, making the future uncertain. Democrats need a new economic plan that works no matter what happens. This plan should focus on abundance, government ownership, and supporting human work.
articles.reason: Política económica pro-abundancia para era IA

articles.title: Generative AI in German firms: Diffusion, costs, and expected economic effects
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Generative AI use in German firms is growing fast, with more companies adopting it and using it more over time. Firms are spending more on GenAI, expecting it to boost productivity and increase demand for high-skill jobs. Most firms see positive effects on productivity and wages, though early adoption can have some costs before benefits appear.

articles.title: The remote work premium reconsidered
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Remote work is often seen as a valuable job perk, but its benefits largely come from higher pay and better work environments, not just working from home. The impact of remote work varies by job type and manager quality, working well in independent roles but less so in jobs needing close teamwork. Overall, job satisfaction depends more on how work is managed and rewarded than on where employees do their work.
articles.reason: Desentrañando el verdadero valor del trabajo remoto

articles.title: The Fall of the Nerds
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: AI is changing software jobs by making coding easier and cheaper, even for beginners. This is causing software company stocks to crash because their old business models may not work anymore. The shift could change many technical jobs and the economy in big ways.
articles.reason: IA democratiza programación y transforma empleo

articles.title: Measuring national technological trajectories using 200 years of international patent data
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: This study uses 200 years of international patent data to track how countries innovate over time. It shows that strong innovation links to higher growth and depends on investments in R&D, education, and defense. The method compares patent filings across multiple countries to measure true technological progress.
articles.reason: Medición de trayectorias nacionales de innovación

articles.title: Links and short notes, 2026-02-20
articles.author: Jason Crawford
articles.summary: AI technology is advancing quickly, with new systems able to solve complex problems and improve programming. Experts warn we are not ready for the risks AI might bring and call for better security and institutions. There is concern that strict AI regulation could harm creativity and slow progress.
articles.reason: Avances IA y llamada a preparación

articles.title: International financial anarchy
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: The world is moving away from using the U.S. dollar as the main reserve currency and buying more gold instead. This shift is driven by fears about U.S. policies and financial instability. China is buying a lot of gold, possibly to help make its own currency more important in the future.

articles.title: Por qué España está peor preparada para una crisis de precios que la mayoría de países europeos
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El Gobierno insiste en que España parte de una posición ventajosa gracias a los menores precios de la electricidad en el mercado mayorista. Sin embargo, los datos de inflación revelan dificultades

articles.title: Industrial policies, global imbalances, and technological hegemony
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Industrial policies help some countries, like China, grow their high-tech sectors and trade surpluses. These trade surpluses cause capital to flow to deficit countries, which may hurt their innovation and productivity over time. To avoid this, deficit countries should use policies that boost innovation and save more to reduce trade deficits and improve growth.
articles.reason: Políticas industriales y flujos de capital global

articles.title: Roundup #78: Roboliberalism
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: We don’t yet know if AI caused a real productivity boom in 2025 because the data is mixed and other factors like immigration changes matter. Some studies show AI can boost productivity without reducing jobs, while others find unclear effects. Also, many claims about immigration harming native wages are flawed due to poor methods and data mistakes.
articles.reason: IA y productividad: separando señal de ruido

articles.title: Americans are ten times more likely to be fired than Germans
articles.author: Pieter Garicano
articles.summary: Europe is much harder to fire workers from than the United States, which makes companies cautious about hiring and innovating. High costs of layoffs push European businesses to avoid risky new projects, slowing down big innovations like electric cars. To compete globally, Europe may need to reform its job rules while still protecting workers.

articles.title: Pricing cascades: Inflation in a networked economy
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Inflation rose sharply after the pandemic because price increases in key sectors spread through the economy’s supply network, causing many firms to raise prices in a chain reaction. Firms adjust prices only when it’s worth the effort, so supply shocks lead to big, fast inflation spikes, while demand shocks cause slower price changes. Understanding these networks helps explain recent inflation and shows why policies must consider supply chains, not just overall demand.

articles.title: AI is already 10x-ing academic research. How do we get to 100x?
articles.author: Andy Hall
articles.summary: Agentic AI could unlock social science abundance

articles.title: Save us, Digital Cronkite!
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: Social media spreads anger and division because negative content gets more attention and sharing. This has made political talk more extreme and less friendly over time. AI could help fix this by encouraging calm, balanced conversations and bringing people closer to agreement.
articles.reason: IA como solución a polarización mediática

articles.title: Nexo Europa (nº 273) - Avestruces europeas en Irán
articles.author: Nacho Alarcón
articles.summary: The recent attack by the US and Israel on Iran has caused a big crisis in the Middle East, affecting Europe's security and energy. European countries are divided in their response, with some supporting the US and others staying quiet or opposing. The EU is also pushing to buy more European-made products to protect its industries during this unstable time.

articles.title: Issue 22: Why Europe doesn’t have a Tesla
articles.author: Works in Progress
articles.summary: Europe lacks a Tesla because strict labor laws make companies afraid to take big risks. Some countries like Denmark find better ways to balance worker protection and innovation. Works in Progress Issue 22 also explores vaccines, long-lived animals, city growth, and other topics.

articles.title: The market value of non-degree credentials: New evidence from 37 million US workers
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Non-degree credentials like certificates and badges can help workers earn more money if they match the job they do. These credentials give the biggest benefits to people without college degrees and those early in their careers. However, many workers who need these credentials most face barriers, and better information and quality control are needed to make the system fair and effective.

articles.title: El INE revisa una décima al alza la inflación hasta el 3,4%, con subidas de precios generalizadas
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Estadística sube en dos décimas hasta el 2,9% la inflación subyacente, que excluye el precio de la energía y los alimentos frescos, lo que indica que la inflación está siendo generalizada

articles.title: How generational turnover and party switching reshape the US political map
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Political polarization in the US is increasing, with voters sorting themselves into more politically uniform areas across many geographic levels. This rise in segregation is driven mainly by generational turnover in Democratic-leaning areas and party switching in Republican-leaning areas, not by people moving to live near like-minded neighbors. The trend is strongest among white voters and younger generations, suggesting political divides may continue to grow.

articles.title: What a liberal immigration enforcement policy might look like
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: Americans want immigration laws enforced but without harsh tactics like mass deportations. Democrats should create a fair policy that respects the law and is humane to immigrants. They must also work with local authorities and change asylum rules to reduce illegal immigration.

articles.title: Something feels weird about this economy
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: The U.S. economy is doing okay overall, with steady growth and low inflation. Productivity is growing fast, especially in manufacturing and data centers, likely due to AI and more intensive machine use. However, job growth is very weak, which makes the economy feel unusual.
articles.reason: Productividad impulsada por IA e intensificación

articles.title: Democrats need to think bigger on utilities
articles.author: Matthew Yglesias
articles.summary: Democrats should think bigger about electricity and plan for more power generation, not less. Lowering utility profits may cut bills but also reduces needed investments in new energy infrastructure. We need better rules and funding to build more electricity capacity and support economic growth and electrification.

articles.title: What if a few AI companies end up with all the money and power?
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: Contemplating the grim future of the "Robot Lords".

articles.title: Cuentas generacionales de los miembros de los hogares (Cuentas Nacionales de Transferencia para España, 2022)
articles.author: Fedea – Fundación de Estudios de Economía Aplicada
articles.summary: This study analyzes how people of different ages and sexes in Spain finance their consumption through work income, public and private transfers, and savings. It highlights three key redistribution pillars: public sector pensions and services, private family transfers especially to young people, and asset-based income for older adults. The research also shows how taxes and benefits vary by age, affecting intergenerational equity.

articles.title: ¿Dónde se ubican las comunidades de inmigrantes que llegan a Barcelona?
articles.author: admin
articles.summary: Many immigrant communities in Barcelona live in separate neighborhoods, with non-European groups like Pakistanis and Moroccans often in peripheral areas, while Europeans like Italians stay near the city center. Factors such as rental housing, public transport access, and housing types strongly influence where immigrants settle. To reduce segregation, policies should improve public transport and promote affordable social housing for different communities.

articles.title: AI-Powered Skill Classification: Mapping Technology Intensity in the German Labor Market
articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc
articles.summary: This paper creates a new way to measure how much technology is used in jobs in Germany. It uses AI to classify skills into manual, digital, and frontier technology types. The study finds that jobs are moving more toward frontier technologies, especially in specialized roles.
articles.reason: Clasificación IA de intensidad tecnológica laboral

articles.title: Why America's AI Discourse Feels So Broken
articles.author: Derek Thompson
articles.summary: People have very different views about AI depending on where they live and their experiences with the technology. Some believe AI will change jobs and the economy a lot, while others see its impact as slow or limited. The future of AI and work is uncertain, with both risks and opportunities ahead.

articles.title: Trump on Tariffs and Investment
articles.author: John H. Cochrane
articles.summary: President Trump claims his tariffs helped reduce trade deficits and boost investment in the U.S. by encouraging foreign companies to invest more at home. However, economists question whether tariffs truly promote growth or just shift investment locations due to trade and budget constraints. There are also concerns that tariffs strain international alliances and may be hard to remove once in place.

articles.title: An Olympic opportunity for social housing policy: Lessons from the Athens 2004 Olympic Village
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Delivered by PolitePaul service

articles.title: Rapid technology creation widened inequality across time and space
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Delivered by PolitePaul service

articles.title: El Banco de España sube una décima su previsión de crecimiento pese al impacto de la guerra
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El Banco de España ha mejorado su previsión de crecimiento para 2026, pese a la guerra en Oriente Medio, y espera un aumento del PIB del 2,3%. El paquete de medidas anticrisis del Gobierno ayudará a compensar el impacto negativo del conflicto y a contener la inflación. Aunque hay riesgos por el precio del petróleo, la economía española se mantiene sólida y evitará una recesión según el Banco de España.

articles.title: How AI is affecting productivity and jobs in Europe
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: AI adoption increases productivity in European firms by about 4%, especially in medium and large companies. Smaller firms and less developed countries lag behind due to fewer investments in technology and training. To get the most from AI, firms must invest in skills and infrastructure alongside the technology.

articles.title: Sistemas electorales y políticas migratorias
articles.author: admin
articles.summary: Electoral systems shape immigration policies by influencing which candidates compete and win power. Systems needing a majority vote promote broader coalitions and more open immigration policies. Simple plurality systems encourage anti-immigration candidates and lead to stricter policies.

articles.title: La naturaleza precaria de la ventaja comparativa humana frente al agente de inteligencia artificial
articles.author: Antonio Ortiz
articles.summary: The paper discusses how automation with AI is becoming cheap, but verifying its results remains costly and difficult. This creates risks, especially when errors take a long time to appear or are hard to detect. Humans keep an advantage as decision-makers, verifiers, and creators in complex or unmeasurable areas.

articles.title: The State of the Union’s housing non-moment
articles.author: Halina Bennet
articles.summary: Housing affordability is a major economic and political issue today. The president blamed investors for housing problems in his State of the Union speech. However, he avoided deeper solutions to fix the housing crisis.

articles.title: La gasolina está subiendo más que el petróleo en esta crisis y la explicación viene de China
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: The price of gasoline is rising much faster than crude oil due to China's export restrictions on refined fuels. China buys cheap crude from sanctioned countries, refines it, and then limits exports to protect its own supply. This causes gasoline prices worldwide to jump, even though crude oil supply remains stable.

articles.title: The venture capital challenge for Europe
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Venture capital investment in Europe recovered slightly in 2025 but still lags behind the US and other regions. Europe's venture sector struggles due to low returns, market fragmentation, and scattered funding efforts. To grow, Europe needs focused clusters, better public markets, and policies that support entrepreneurs and investors.

articles.title: Saying what we mean about ICE
articles.author: Matthew Yglesias
articles.summary: Hakeem Jeffries has led House Democrats well by using new tactics like discharge petitions to challenge Republicans. Immigration protests should focus on clear goals, like holding ICE accountable, rather than extreme demands that divide people. Most agree that bad actions by Trump’s administration are wrong, and protests should reflect that shared concern.

articles.title: Nexo Europa (nº 272) - Caos comercial
articles.author: Nacho Alarcón
articles.summary: The US Supreme Court struck down Trump's reciprocal tariffs, causing new trade tensions. The European Commission urges clarity and legal certainty for European businesses amid these changes. Meanwhile, political and diplomatic challenges continue between the US, Europe, and other global players.

articles.title: EU capital markets reform should focus on innovation investment
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: The EU lacks a strong and integrated venture capital market, especially for growing innovative companies. This limits funding for scale-up firms and causes many successful startups to leave Europe. To fix this, experts propose six steps to boost investment, reduce market barriers, and keep firms and money within the EU.

articles.title: Designing Gender-Balanced Evaluation Committees with AI
articles.author: Fedea – Fundación de Estudios de Economía Aplicada
articles.summary: This study uses AI to measure how research topics differ between male and female economists. It introduces the Gender-Topic Alignment Index (GTAI) to create balanced evaluation committees based on these topic differences. The goal is to ensure fairer and more gender-neutral research evaluations.

articles.title: An end to cervical cancer is possible
articles.author: Hannah Ritchie
articles.summary: And some countries are already on track to do so.

articles.title: Physician Competition: Entry and Substitution -- by Joshua D. Gottlieb, Sean Nicholson
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: Physician entry is limited by strict caps on medical training slots, especially for high-paying specialties. More nurse practitioners and physician assistants are now competing with doctors for patients. To understand competition in healthcare, we must consider rules about who can provide care, not just market concentration.
articles.reason: Competencia y sustitución en mercados médicos

articles.title: Why Sweden has so many unicorns
articles.author: Luis Garicano
articles.summary: Sweden has many unicorns because it has lots of promising startups, not just more venture capital. Successful entrepreneurs often become angel investors, creating a cycle that supports new companies. Government incentives work best when they encourage experienced investors, not just increase funding.

articles.title: Residential Segregation and Unequal Access to Local Public Services in India: Evidence from 1.5m Neighborhoods -- by Sam Asher, Kritarth Jha, Paul Novosad, Anjali Adukia, Brandon Tan
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: The study looks at segregation and public service access in 1.5 million neighborhoods in India. Muslim and Scheduled Caste areas have less access to facilities than others. These inequalities happen mostly within cities and are hidden in big-picture data.

articles.title: The AI Vibe Shift Has Officially Arrived
articles.author: Derek Thompson
articles.summary: We seem to be moving from a period of demand scarcity (not enough customers) to supply scarcity (not enough compute)
articles.reason: Cambio de demanda a escasez compute IA

articles.title: A wartime labour market: The case of Ukraine
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: The war in Ukraine caused a huge loss of workers and changed where and how people work. Despite these challenges, the labor market showed surprising strength, with unemployment dropping and wages rising by 2025. To rebuild, Ukraine must support veterans, invest in education, include more diverse workers, and encourage refugees and displaced people to return.
articles.reason: Adaptación laboral en economía de guerra

articles.title: Refine
articles.author: John H. Cochrane
articles.summary: John H. Cochrane tested an AI tool called refine that improved his academic paper on inflation with sharp, clear feedback. He found the AI's comments as good as the best human reviewers and believes it will save economists a lot of time. Cochrane encourages others to start using AI tools now to improve writing and research efficiency.

articles.title: El INE confirma que el PIB de España creció un 2,8% en 2025, el doble que el de la eurozona
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El PIB de España creció un 2,8% en 2025, casi el doble que la eurozona. Este crecimiento se debe principalmente al consumo interno y a una fuerte inversión. Aunque la productividad mejoró, su avance se ha frenado en los últimos meses.

articles.title: Roundup #77: The Fix-Everything Button
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: AI is not clearly causing job losses for young college graduates; economic factors play a big role. Tariffs have not reduced America's overall trade deficit but have helped separate the U.S. and China economically. U.S. visa restrictions are pushing tech companies to hire more workers overseas, especially in India.

articles.title: Don’t let Elon Musk monopolize space compute
articles.author: Matthew Yglesias
articles.summary: Elon Musk wants to merge his space launch company with his AI business, risking a monopoly in space-based computing. This could harm competition and innovation, so the merger should be blocked or strictly regulated. Rules should ensure all companies can use space launch services fairly, preventing Musk from gaining unfair advantages.

articles.title: España tendrá menos deuda pública que la media mundial por primera vez en 16 años
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La dinámica de gasto de los últimos años, el rápido aumento de la recaudación y el crecimiento económico hacen que España sea uno de los países que más se está desapalancando

articles.title: The Most Important Chart in AI Is Also the Most Misunderstood
articles.author: Derek Thompson
articles.summary: A popular chart shows AI is improving very fast at tasks humans do. But many people misunderstand what the chart really means. The chart does not prove AI will take over all human jobs soon.
articles.reason: Malinterpretación de progreso en capacidades IA

articles.title: What Happened With Bio Anchors?
articles.author: Scott Alexander
articles.summary: Ajeya Cotra’s Bio Anchors report predicted AI progress and AGI timelines around the 2050s but underestimated how fast AI would improve. The report relied on estimates of compute needs and algorithmic progress, but actual AI advances have been much faster than expected. Updating key assumptions, especially about algorithmic progress, gives forecasts closer to current AI developments and earlier AGI predictions.

articles.title: La imposibilidad de eliminar la discriminación estadística
articles.author: José Luis Ferreira
articles.summary: Discriminación estadística ocurre cuando se usa información grupal para evaluar a personas, lo que puede ser injusto pero a veces necesario por limitaciones en la información. Mejorar la calidad de los datos puede reducir esta discriminación, pero nunca eliminarla completamente debido a un teorema de imposibilidad. El reto ético es cómo tratar a cada persona como individuo sin depender solo de características grupales, algo que solo es posible con información perfecta.

articles.title: America should be less involved in the Middle East
articles.author: Matthew Yglesias
articles.summary: The United States should reduce its involvement in Middle East conflicts, especially with Iran. America's interests in the region are limited and different from those of local countries like Israel and Saudi Arabia. Instead of fighting regional wars, the U.S. should focus on global priorities and avoid unnecessary risks.

articles.title: A silver lining to the European energy crisis: Energy efficiency, productivity, and potential output
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: The European energy crisis led to higher energy prices, which encouraged firms to improve energy efficiency and innovate in energy-saving technologies. This helped reduce natural gas use and limited the drop in potential economic output. Policies should focus on stabilizing energy prices and supporting energy-efficient innovation to strengthen the economy.

articles.title: Documentos de Trabajo. 2610. Stimulating avenues: EIB loans and returns to Public Investment
articles.author: Últimas publicaciones - Publicaciones - Banco de España
articles.summary: This study looks at how public investment affects the economy using European data and loans from the European Investment Bank. Public investment boosts jobs and production without hurting private investment, consumption, or causing inflation. After five years, the economic output increases significantly, especially when credit conditions are good.

articles.title: Inmigración, Envejecimiento y Dividendo Demográfico: El caso de España en perspectiva
articles.author: Fedea – Fundación de Estudios de Economía Aplicada
articles.summary: Immigration has helped reduce the negative impact of Spain's aging population on economic growth since 2000. Without immigration, the working-age population would have declined faster, hurting income per person. Future migration is expected to continue easing demographic challenges, supporting Spain's economic growth.

articles.title: Nexo Europa (nº 269) - Contra la competitividad
articles.author: Nacho Alarcón
articles.summary: The article questions the European focus on "competitiveness," arguing it oversimplifies complex economic issues. It highlights Europe's strengths in education, clean technology, and macroeconomic stability, while pointing out areas needing improvement like financial markets and business dynamism. The author believes political attention should shift away from competitiveness talk toward real actions that benefit citizens.

articles.title: Mind the Gap: AI Adoption in Europe and the US
articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc
articles.summary: Delivered by PolitePaul service

articles.title: How sanctions can help stabilise global oil supply
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Sanctions on oil-exporting countries can lower global oil prices and stabilize supply if designed well. The Russian oil price cap shows that limiting prices can encourage producers to sell more oil now rather than later. Strong enforcement is key to making sanctions effective without causing market disruption.
articles.reason: Sanciones como estabilizador de suministro energético

articles.title: La inmigración salva a 38 provincias de perder población, incluyendo Madrid y Barcelona
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Immigration is helping 38 Spanish provinces, including Madrid and Barcelona, avoid population loss. Most immigrants are young adults who join the workforce and help balance Spain's aging population. Meanwhile, many young Spaniards move away due to high housing costs, changing the country's population structure.

articles.title: Life-Cycle Effects of Women's Education on their Careers and Children -- by Na'ama Shenhav, Danielle H. Sandler
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: Women who complete some college have better jobs and earn more money over their lives. Their work is also more meaningful and has better conditions. These career gains help improve their children's early health and well-being.

articles.title: España es el país europeo con más porcentaje de recién nacidos de madres de 40 años o más
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Spain has the highest percentage of babies born to mothers aged 40 or older in Europe, mainly due to economic difficulties. Young people delay having children because housing is expensive and salaries are low. This trend lowers birth rates and poses a future challenge for Spain.

articles.title: Inmigración y ciclo económico en España: cuando la movilidad importa
articles.author: admin
articles.summary: Immigration flows in Spain rise during economic growth and fall in recessions, affecting the labor market differently depending on the cycle. Immigrants face higher unemployment in downturns, but their mobility helps reduce job competition and eases native unemployment. Understanding this cyclical impact is key to making better migration policies.

articles.title: The Takaichi Era begins for real
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: Japan's ruling party, the LDP, won a record majority with new allies, helping Prime Minister Takaichi strengthen the country amid rising regional threats. Japan faces big challenges like a weak military industry, heavy debt, and reliance on the US for defense against China. To stay safe, Japan plans to boost defense spending, build new alliances, and modernize its technology and economy.

articles.title: A consensus–but it’s a consensus of uncertainty.
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: Jay Kadane argues that seeking consensus is not always good, especially when data are uncertain. In his hurricane study, different assumptions led to different conclusions, so the best consensus is admitting we don’t know. Similarly, in elections, acknowledging uncertainty can be informative, even if some people prefer clear answers.

articles.author: Nate Silver
articles.summary: The 2026 women's March Madness is expected to be dominated by the top four #1 seeds, especially UConn, which has a 35% chance to win the championship. Upsets are much less likely in the women's tournament compared to the men's, with top teams often winning by large margins. Fans can look forward to exciting matchups, including potential clashes between UCLA, Texas, South Carolina, and UConn later in the tournament.

articles.author: Scott Alexander

articles.title: Democrats don’t need a Project 2029
articles.author: Matthew Yglesias
articles.summary: The author thinks Project 2029 is a bad idea because a similar plan, Project 2025, failed politically. Democrats don’t need a secret project because progressive ideas already influence their leaders. Instead of copying Project 2025, they should focus on practical and popular policies.

articles.title: Republicans are complaining about a redistricting war they started
articles.author: Halina Bennet
articles.summary: Republicans started a fight over redistricting. A judge in Virginia stopped Democrats from changing some voting districts. This pause helped the GOP in the ongoing national dispute.

articles.title: Nearly a week out from Texas
articles.author: Halina Bennet
articles.summary: A new poll shows only 36% of Americans approve of President Trump's job. His support has dropped in nearly all groups since he took office. This highlights growing dissatisfaction with his leadership.

articles.title: Open Thread 421
articles.author: Scott Alexander
articles.summary: This is a weekly open thread where people can post anything or ask questions. The site has a subreddit, Discord, bulletin board, and meetups worldwide. Some content is free, and some requires a subscription.

articles.title: The fifth annual Cherry Blossom Prediction Competition is open!
articles.author: Lizzie
articles.summary: The fifth annual Cherry Blossom Prediction Competition is now open to everyone. A hackathon called ‘Petals and Probabilities’ will be held on February 21, 2026, at Georgetown University for students to predict cherry blossom bloom dates. The event includes a guest speaker, free food, prizes, and opportunities for networking.

articles.title: My book is published in the US today, and I'm shortlisted for the Unwin Award
articles.author: Hannah Ritchie
articles.summary: Hannah Ritchie's book, "Clearing the Air," is now published in the US and Canada. She is also shortlisted for the Unwin Award, which honors promising non-fiction authors. She thanks readers for their feedback on her Energy Comparison Tool and plans to improve it soon.

articles.title: Boletín Económico del BCE. Número 1/2026
articles.author: Últimas publicaciones - Publicaciones - Banco de España
articles.summary: Banco Central Europeo. Traducción: Banco de España

articles.title: Hey!  I almost got scammed by Google.
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: Andrew tried to renew his passport online and almost paid a scam website because it was a .com, not a .gov site. He realized the mistake in time and used the official site instead, but the scam site got his personal info. Even being internet-savvy, Andrew warns that scams can still trick you.

articles.title: La partición de la Gran Hungría y la cartografía del dolor
articles.author: Miguel García Álvarez
articles.summary: The Treaty of Trianon in 1920 greatly reduced Hungary's territory, leaving many Hungarians outside its new borders. Hungarian nationalism strongly opposed this loss and created propaganda maps to show their claim to these lands. Despite repression after World War II, the memory of Greater Hungary remains in some parts of the country today.

articles.title: More cows, more wives
articles.author: Works in Progress
articles.summary: Most societies in history have practiced polygamy, not the lifelong monogamous marriage common in Europe. Farming and wealth led to men having multiple wives and passing property mainly to sons, increasing inequality. Today, marriage systems are changing again, balancing between stability, freedom, and how people share wealth and family roles.

articles.title: Axel F meets Samuel Beckett in the world’s most pointless conversation
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: The author found a strange and confusing conversation involving Richard Axel and others. The talk jumps between science, biology, and random topics, feeling pointless like a play. Despite this, the author enjoyed the unusual exchange and its quirky moments.

articles.title: Luxury goods are Europe’s global tax on vanity
articles.author: The Economist
articles.summary: Europe makes a lot of money by selling luxury goods. These expensive items show off status and attract rich buyers worldwide. Even when the economy is weak, luxury sales stay strong.

articles.title: En el mercado de las ideas, los anti inteligencia artificial van ganando
articles.author: Antonio Ortiz
articles.summary: En pocos días se han cruzado dos declaraciones.

articles.title: The European Onion is a joke whose time has come
articles.author: The Economist
articles.summary: Europe has tried many ways to unite over the years. Instead of one big union, a new idea called the European Onion has appeared. This model offers a different way for countries to work together.

articles.title: Markets are churning furiously beneath a calm surface
articles.author: The Economist
articles.summary: Markets look calm, but big changes are happening underneath. AI is making investors rethink many business models. Despite global problems, the S&P 500 stays near its record high.

articles.title: What drives the wage gap between men and women?
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: The wage gap between men and women is mainly caused by motherhood. Women often earn less because taking care of children affects their work. This shows that family responsibilities still shape pay differences today.

articles.title: From junk science (largely non-political) to junk medical treatments (mostly associated with the far-right):  A financial connection
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: There is a strong financial link between junk medical treatments and right-wing extremism. Wellness and supplement industries profit from promoting false health claims. This relationship helps spread anti-scientific ideas and political influence.

articles.title: The alpha political male
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Mario Carillo studies how voters choose alpha males as leaders and how these leaders behave in power. The research explores the impact of alpha males leading countries. The discussion also links populism to conflict and war.

articles.title: Malicious Streetlight Effects Vs. "Directional Correctness" - A Semi-Non-Apology
articles.author: Scott Alexander
articles.summary: Malicious streetlight effects happen when people use true facts about a similar issue to dismiss a valid complaint about a different issue. The author struggles to correct false claims about crime rates without upsetting those concerned about disorder and minor crimes. He plans to address crime and disorder separately to better handle these complex debates.

articles.title: What Kind Of Apes Are We?
articles.author: David Pinsof
articles.summary: The author argues that humans are mostly rational and shaped by evolution, not confused or maladapted as some intellectuals claim. He believes intellectuals often overstate their role in fixing the world and are motivated by prestige more than real change. The author and his respondent mostly agree but differ on how much emphasis to place on human flaws versus strengths.

articles.title: Will Female Graduates Break Chinese Patriarchy?
articles.author: Alice Evans
articles.summary: Female education is often sold as the great equaliser. But are institutions really so meritocratic?

articles.title: Where should Pablo Picasso’s “Guernica” be allowed to hang?
articles.author: Europe
articles.summary: Arguments over the painting’s rightful place hark back to ugly regional politics

articles.author: Andrew
